coredata.dc:title: A carbon dots-based coating for the determination of phthalate esters by solid-phase microextraction coupled gas chromatography in water samples
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Dechang
Chen, Xiulin
Liu, Fu
Wu, Jian-Feng
Zhao, Guang-Chao
coredata.dc:description: A rapid and facile route is followed to synthesize carbon dots (CDs) immobilized on the surface of steel wire from candle soot (CS) for the efficient and selective determination of phthalate esters (PAEs) as solid phase micro-extraction (SPME) coating. Five PAEs were selected as target analytes and detected through a head-space SPME mode coupling with gas chromatography-flame ionization detection (GC-FID) from aqueous samples. The effects of various factors on the extraction efficiency, such as extraction time, extraction temperature, salt content and stirring rate, were optimized through orthogonal experiment. Under the optimal conditions, the fiber showed a good linear in the concentration range of 1.0–500 μg·L−1 (R2 > 0.9909). The limit of detections (LODs) and quantifications (LOQs) ranged from 0.32 to 3.13 μg·L−1 and 1.07–10.43 for five PAEs, respectively. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of intra-day and inter-day for these PAEs at a concentration level of 10 μg·L−1 (n = 5) were 3.7–8.6% and 5.6–13.3%, respectively. The prepared fiber can be repeatedly used more than 50 times, showing a long lifetime and good stability. The proposed method had been successfully applied to the analysis of PAEs in real water samples with recoveries varied between 82 and 119%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Solid-phase microextraction
Phthalate esters
Determination

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of the mode of extraction
Optimization of the conditions of extraction
Comparison of the carbon dots fiber with the epoxy resin fiber

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors appreciate the support of the Research Innovation and Practice of Anhui Normal University in 2016 (No. 2016yks074). Author Xiulin Chen declares that he has no conflict of interest. Author Tong Tian declares that she has no conflict of interest. Author Jianfeng Wu declares that he has no conflict of interest. Author Guang-Chao Zhao declares that he has no conflict of interest. This article does not contain any studies with human participants or animals performed by any of the authors.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectrum of carbon dots after conditioned at 250 °C.

SEM images of the surface of fiber coating. (a) Stainless steel wire coated by carbon dots, (b) the amplified image of (a), (c) stainless steel wire coated only by epoxy resin, (d) the amplified image of (c).
Analysis of extraction experimental results of DEP by response surface method.
Effect of two fibers on extraction efficiency. Conditions: extraction time, 60 min; extraction temperature, 80 °C; salt of concentration, 12%; stirring rate, 300 rpm; sample level, 10 μg·L−1.

Chromatograms of the PAEs extracted by carbon dots fiber, 1-BBP, 2-DMP, 3-DEP, 4-DBP, 5-DNOP. (a)Yangtze River water sample, (b) Yangtze River water sample spiked with 5 μg·L−1 PAEs, and (c) Yangtze River water sample spiked with 10 μgL−1 PAEs. Extraction conditions: Sample volume, 5 mL; extraction time, 60 min; salt of concentration, 16% (w/v), extraction temperature, 80 °C; stirring rate, 500 rpm; desorption time, 5 min.
The optimal extraction conditions for all analytes.
The extraction efficiency of two fibers.
Analytical parameters of the proposed method (n = 5).
Comparison of different fibers of SPME for PAEs.
Analytical results for determination of PAEs in water samples.

head.ce:title: A carbon dots-based coating for the determination of phthalate esters by solid-phase microextraction coupled gas chromatography in water samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Anhui Normal University, Wuhu, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Anhui Normal University, Wuhu, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A rapid and facile route is followed to synthesize carbon dots (CDs) immobilized on the surface of steel wire from candle soot (CS) for the efficient and selective determination of phthalate esters (PAEs) as solid phase micro-extraction (SPME) coating. Five PAEs were selected as target analytes and detected through a head-space SPME mode coupling with gas chromatography-flame ionization detection (GC-FID) from aqueous samples. The effects of various factors on the extraction efficiency, such as extraction time, extraction temperature, salt content and stirring rate, were optimized through orthogonal experiment. Under the optimal conditions, the fiber showed a good linear in the concentration range of 1.0–500 μg·L−1 (R2 > 0.9909). The limit of detections (LODs) and quantifications (LOQs) ranged from 0.32 to 3.13 μg·L−1 and 1.07–10.43 for five PAEs, respectively. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of intra-day and inter-day for these PAEs at a concentration level of 10 μg·L−1 (n = 5) were 3.7–8.6% and 5.6–13.3%, respectively. The prepared fiber can be repeatedly used more than 50 times, showing a long lifetime and good stability. The proposed method had been successfully applied to the analysis of PAEs in real water samples with recoveries varied between 82 and 119%.

ce:section.ce:para: There is no doubt today thataddition of phthalate esters (PAEs) can increase the mechanical properties of plastic materials with the advantages of economy and practical efficiency [1]. However it was reported that their degradation products and metabolites had an serious harm on human health, including on the liver, kidney, testicles and nerve [2]. Moreover, it had been also reported the extensive use of plastic products accelerate to the PAEs transferring from plastic products into water environment [3]. Now they were considered to one of the main sources of persistent organic pollutions (POPs) and some typical PAEs (e.g. di-(2-ethylhexyl)phthalate (DEHP), di-n-butyl phthalate (DBP) and benzyl butyl phthalate (BBP), etc.) had appeared in the priority list released by EU [4] and USA [5]. Although the number of typical residual PAEs are more than 17 species, the concentration of most of them maintain in the ranged of trace levels in the environment [6,7]. Monitoring PAEs concentrations in environment and food samples is important to protect human health and safety. Because of the low concentration and complex matrices of PAEs, sample pretreatment meeting the requirement of instrumental sensitivity is of great necessary prior to their instrumental analysis.

The pretreatment methods for detecting PAEs in water samples are usually included Soxhlet extraction [8], liquid–liquid micro-extraction (LLME) [9], micro-solid phase extraction (μ-SPE) [10], solid phase extraction (SPE) [11], low-density solvent-based vortex-assisted surfactant-enhanced-emulsification liquid–liquid microextraction (LDS-VSLLME) [12], bell-shaped extraction device assisted liquid–liquid microextraction (BSED-LLME) [13], vortex solvent bar microextraction (VSBME) [14], and solidification of floating drop microextraction (SFDME) [15]. However, it seems that these techniques have also many disadvantages such as high cost, complex operation, organic solvents needing, time and labor consuming, especially to study on trace analytes in complex matrix. For this purpose, solid phase microextraction (SPME), a solvent-free sample preparation technique, had also been applied for the detection of trace PAEs [16–20]. It was realized that SPME possesses some significant advantages including simpleness, speediness, green and low cost. Hence it has been widely introduced for the determination of different organic compounds in the environment and food fields [21].

In SPME, the coating is the core for the sensitivity and selectivity of detection analytes, therefore the selection of a suitable coating material to prepare the SPME fiber is the key. To improve the extraction effects, various coating materials with remarkable properties have been developed [22–26]. Carbon materials have attracted wide interests in SPME due to the highspecificsurfacearea, aromatic layer structure and porosity. Therefore, grapheme [27], carbon nanotubes [28], carbon monolith [29], pencil lead [30,31] etc. had received extensive attention by researchers. Recently, in the family of carbon materials, carbon dot starts to attract increasing interests in the field of materials science.

Carbon dots [32], a new member of carbon family following graphene and carbon nanotubes, was first reported in 2004. The core of carbon dots is three-dimensional carbon cluster, consisting of sp2 hybrid nanocrystalline carbon or amorphous carbon with size in the range of 2–10 nm. Carbon dots were often used as fluorescent probe for chemical and medical field because of their excellent fluorescence characteristics [33]. Carbon dots had shown the excellent adsorption properties for its large specific surface area and some polar functional groups, such as carboxyl, carbonyl, lactone, acid anhydride, pyrone and so on. Like other carbon materials, carbon dots have revealed enormous potential as an excellent coating in SPME due to their high adsorption capacity, good thermal stability, corrosion resistance, uniform porosity and non-toxicity.

In this paper, the prepared carbon dots resulting from candle soot were selected as a coating material to fabricate SPME fiber by dip method [34–36]. The fiber was successfully used for extracting five phthalate esters, including butyl benzyl phthalate (BBP), Di-n-butyl phthalate (DBP), dimethyl phthalate (DMP), diethyl phthalate (DEP) and di-n-octyl phthalate (DNOP), from aqueous solution with headspace SPME mode. The performances of as-prepared carbon dots-based SPME fiber were investigated.

Five PAEs compounds including BBP, DBP, DMP, DEP and DNOP with 2.0 mg·mL−1 in isooctane was purchased from Shanghai j&k chemical Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The PAEs stock solution with 104 μg·L−1 of each compound was prepared in acetone solution. All PAEs stock and working solutions were stored in the dark at 4 °C. Epoxy resin structural adhesive A-200NL was gained from Kisling Ltd. (Switzerland, Germany). Other chemical reagents are of analytical grade. And ultrapure water was used throughout this work.
Water samples were collected from a river in the east of the industrial area and Yangtze River in Wuhu.

Hitachi S-4800 scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Kyoto, Japan) was used to observe the morphological characters of fibers. The functional groups of carbon dots were recorded by Fourier transform infrared spectrometer 8400 s (FTIR, Shimadzu, Japan).

Analysis of the model compounds was performed with a GC-2010 gas chromatography (Shimadzu, Japan) equipped with a flame ionization detector (FID). The column for the determination was an RTX-5 capillary column (Restek, USA) (30.0 m × 0.25 mm id. × 0.25 μm film thickness).

The conditions of the determination are described as following: The carrier gas is nitrogen (purity > 99.999%) and flow rate is 1.51 mL·min−1. The flow rate of ultrapure hydrogen (purity > 99.999%) is kept at 40.0 mL·min−1 and that of air, coming from an automatic air intake device, is 400.0 mLmin−1. The initial column temperature was set at 60 °C for 1.0 min. Then, it was raised up to 220 °C with 20.0 °C min−1 and kept for 1.0 min. Finally, the column temperature was raised up to 280 °C with 5.0 °C min−1 and held for 5.0 min. The injector temperature was fixed at 250 °C in splitless mode while the detector temperature was set at 300 °C.

The carbon dots material was prepared according to previous report with modification [37]. In detail, a candle was ignited for collecting the candle ash under a 50 mL clean beaker, which was put upside down on the iron stand. Then, approximately 0.2 g candle ash was accumulated on the inside of the beaker after the candle burns out. The collected candle ash was transferred into a 250 mL flask which containing 160 mL concentrated sulfuric acid and nitric acid (3:1, v:v). The flask was put into a sonicator for 6 h with the solution temperature 40 °C, and then it was put down in the darkness of 4 °C for 12 h. The precipitated mixture was separated and the pH of the mixture was washed to neutral by ultrapure water. Then, the mixture was dialysis by semipermeable membranethe (MD 44 mm, MWCO: 3500) to obtain the pure carbon dots. Finally, the resulted product of carbon dots was dried in the oven for the further experiments.

The polished stainless steel wire was sonicated for 30 min in acetone, and immersed in 2 mol·L−1 NaOH for 4 h. Next, it was rinsed with ultrapure water and dried at room temperature. The clean surface of the stainless steel wire was firstly coated with a thin layer of epoxy resin [16]. Then, it was inserted quickly into a solution containing certain concentration of carbon dots under a stable, and low stirring rate for 5 min. When above process was accomplished, the fiber was drying at 100 °C for 180 min in the oven. Finally, the dried fiber was installed into a micro-syringe and put into the GC injector to condition for 60 min at 260 °C.

10 mL of sample solution was sealed in a 20 mL sample glass vial capped with a septum. The prepared fiber was set above the solution to extract the target analytes in the mode of head-space. After accomplished the extraction process, the fiber was transferred immediately into the GC injector and held down 5 min to desorb the extracted materials. Then, the target analytes were separated and detected by GC. All the determinations of samples were performed at least three times except for extra explanations, and one blank run of the fiber after every day was performed to minimize carryover effects.

Carbon dot is a kind of carbon nano-material with numerous functional groups on its surface. These characteristics make them suitable as extraction materials to improve the extraction efficiency. The method of carbon dots derived from candle soot possesses higher yield, safer as well as easier operation process, and cheaper raw material comparing with the traditional hydrothermal and solid pyrolysis method. The synthesized carbon dots are easily introduced more oxygen-containing groups contributingto extraction [38,39].

In order to character the functional groups on the surface of carbon dots, the FTIR spectrum of carbon dots was recorded as Fig. 1 . From the spectrum, the main stretching vibration occurs at 3419 cm−1. The peaks at 3419 cm−1 and 1383 cm−1 are both assigned to the O–H bond. The nearby peak at 2924 cm−1 represents C–H stretching vibration, the sharp peak obtained at 1614 cm−1 shows the CC stretching of the aromatic rings while the peak at 1099 cm−1 is associated with the stretching of the C-O-C bond. These special peaks on FTIR revealed that there are lots of active functional groups, such as hydroxyl, alkoxy, carboxyl group etc. on the surface of carbon dots. The numerous O–H bonds are easy to form hydrogen bond between carbon dots and the molecular of PAEs. In addition, a lot of bonds of CC are much possible to form new chemical bond with the group of PAEs. On the other hand, it has been reported that C-O-C bond has unique structure so that it can take place the chelation with the molecular of PAEs [16]. So, the existence of numerous functional groups on the surface of carbon dots is favor to the adsorption of PAEs on carbon dots surface.

Fig. 2 describes the scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of the prepared carbon dots fiber and the only epoxy resin fiber. In Fig. 2, (a) and (c) are the overall morphology of the fibers while (b) and (d) are the amplified image of fibers. On the carbon dots fiber (a), the overall surface of coating is rough and irregular. However, on the epoxy resin fiber surface (c), it looks like very smooth and uniform. From the amplified images, the surface of the carbon dots fiber (b) presents numerous fragmentation together with many semi-exposed carbon dots attached on the surface of coating while the surface of the epoxy resin fiber (d) are still smooth and no significantly rough structure. It is also obvious that the carbon dots fiber has more porosity and larger specific surface area than the epoxy resin one. In addition, it can be estimated that the thickness of carbon dots coating was about 10 μm by measuring the images of carbon dots coating.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the mode of extraction
ce:section.ce:para: PAEs is a series of semi-volatile organic matters with high boiling points (>295 °C), it seems that target analytes could be extracted more better in the direct-immerse mode of SPME. However, some researches about the mode of head-space SPME of phthalate esters are reported [40,6,7]. In this study, we compare peak areas gained from the direct-immerse and head-space modes of SPME. For the both modes, the extraction parameters like extraction temperature, salt concentration and extraction time were selected based on the previous study [16,34]. For direct-immerse SPME, the main effects are extraction temperature and extraction time, etc., extraction temperature was selected ranged from 30 °C to 80 °C, and the extraction time was set from 20 min to 100 min. Unfortunately, the results of direct-immerse mode show that when extraction temperature was rather than 50 °C and extraction time was rather than 40 min, the extraction efficiency of carbon dots-based fiber get worse, even the coating started to drop from stainless steel wire matrix. The results get more terrible with the raise of extraction temperature and longer extraction time. To the contrast, the results of head-space mode show an excellent stability and extraction effect. There are some possible reasons that influence the carbon dots-based fiber about extraction efficiency. On the one hand, the surface of carbon dots possess many active functional groups so that it’s easy to form hydrogen bond with water molecular in water samples, which leads to less contact opportunity between the molecular of PAEs and active adsorption sites. On the other hand, higher temperatures increase water molecular thermal motion and disturbance solution strongly, it’s easier to damage the coating of carbon dots-based fiber in the mode of direct-immerse. However, the mode of head-space could help the carbon dots-based coating avoid these damages. Considering to these results, the mode of head-space of SPME was selected for later experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the conditions of extraction
ce:section.ce:para: In generally, the experimental parameters, such as extraction time and temperature, ion strength, stirring rate and pH, etc., can affect the extraction efficiency in SPME. In order to evaluate the effects of different experimental conditions on extraction target analytes to obtain the optimal extraction conditions, the main factors affecting the extraction efficiency of the carbon dots fiber are investigated by orthogonal experiments. The experimental conditions of the designed orthogonal experiment are listed in Table 1 .

Orthogonal designs (Table S1 of Supplementary material) were carried out by the software of Design-Expert 8.0.6. Then, the typical model of response surface of DEP was established while the experimental results were imported to the software (as shown in Fig. 3 ). Meanwhile, other response surface models by the software to analyze the optimized extraction experimental conditions of carbon dots-based fiber, like BBP, DMP, DBP and DNOP are also established (as shown in Figs. S1–S4 of Supplementary material).

As shown in Fig. 3 and Figs. S1–S4, the ordinate of peak area was higher, the experimental result was better. In other words, the couple of extraction parameter design was more suitable to the carbon dots-based fiber extraction on analytes. What’s more, the red surface represents good extraction effects while blue surface represent bad one. Meanwhile, The optimal extraction conditions were combined for all analytes on the basis of Table 2 from Fig. 3 and Figs. S1–S4. So, we selected the optimum extraction conditions of carbon dots-based fiber as follows: extraction time, 60 min; extraction temperature, 80 °C; ionic strength, 16% and stirring rate, 500 rpm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the carbon dots fiber with the epoxy resin fiber
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the epoxy resin was used as an adhesion agent to adhere carbon dots on the surface of stainless steel wire. Due to be an organic material, it should be able to adsorb the target analytes. In order to test the extraction capability of carbon dots, the comparison of extraction quantity and efficiency of carbon dots-based fiber with the epoxy resin fiber was investigated and the results are shown in Table 3 (The extraction efficiency of carbon fiber is set to 1 as a reference) and Fig. 4 . The extraction efficiency of carbon dots-based fiber is about one time higher than the epoxy resin one for all target analytes. Because of the existent of various functional groups, carbon dots can provide much more adsorption sites for PAEs than epoxy resin. On the other hand, from the SEM images (Fig. 2), the surface of the epoxy resin fiber is smooth while it is rough on carbon dots one, which may results in that carbon dots fiber has a larger specific area than epoxy resin. Therefore, carbon dots-based fiber exhibits a higher extraction capacity.

The developed SPME method was evaluated and the analytical characteristics of the method have been summarized in Table 4 . The limit of detection (LOD, S/N = 3) and limit of quantification (LOQ, S/N = 10) of these PAEs is in the range of 0.32–3.13 μg·L−1 and 1.07–10.43 μg·L−1, respectively. A linear range from 1.0 to 500 μg·L−1 for each PAE was obtained with good linearity (R2 > 0.9909). The relative standard deviations (RSD) of intra-day and inter-day (n = 5) for a single fiber ranges from 3.7 to 8.6% and 5.6 to 13.3%, respectively. The RSD (n = 5) of fiber-to-fiber is in the range of 7.5–15.6%, meaning that the RSDs between fiber and fiber is acceptable. In addition, no significant decrease of extraction effect for each PAE was observed after 50 cycles of extraction, revealing a satisfactory stability for the prepared SPME fiber. Comparing with other methods [41–45,16], the performance of the developed SPME method is acceptable, as shown in Table 5 .

Carbon dot, a new carbon nano-material, reveals excellent characteristics including large specific surface area, high thermal stability, large adsorption capacity, and easy chemical modification, and that is promising as SPME coatings for the extraction of organic compounds. The carbon dot-based coating showed outstanding inter-day and intra-day precision, wide linear range, low limits of detection and high recoveries for the determination of PAEs in water samples. The prepared SPME fiber can be used up to 50 cycles for head-space SPME analysis of PAEs in watermixture, showing a long lifetime and good stability. It can be expected reasonably that the carbon dots-based SPME fibers can also be applied to detect other residual toxic organic contaminant of water and other samples in trace level.

Dechang Wu: Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Xiulin Chen: Conceptualization, Investigation, Validation. Fu Liu: Conceptualization, Investigation, Validation. Jian-Feng Wu: Writing - review & editing. Guang-Chao Zhao: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors appreciate the support of the Research Innovation and Practice of Anhui Normal University in 2016 (No. 2016yks074).

Author Xiulin Chen declares that he has no conflict of interest. Author Tong Tian declares that she has no conflict of interest. Author Jianfeng Wu declares that he has no conflict of interest. Author Guang-Chao Zhao declares that he has no conflict of interest.
This article does not contain any studies with human participants or animals performed by any of the authors.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105563.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
